content.explanation:
The Document Object Model (DOM) is a tree-structured representation of an HTML document that the browser creates when it parses your HTML. Every element, attribute, and piece of text becomes a node in this tree. JavaScript can access and manipulate this tree through the document object, allowing you to dynamically change the content, structure, and styling of a web page without reloading it.

Selecting elements is the first step in any DOM operation. The modern API provides two primary methods: document.querySelector(selector) returns the first element matching a CSS selector, and document.querySelectorAll(selector) returns a static NodeList of all matches. Older methods like getElementById, getElementsByClassName, and getElementsByTagName still work and can be slightly faster for simple lookups, but querySelector's CSS selector syntax is more flexible and consistent. Note that querySelectorAll returns a static snapshot while getElementsBy* methods return live collections that update automatically.

Creating and inserting elements involves document.createElement(tag) to create a new element, setting its properties (textContent, innerHTML, setAttribute, classList), and then inserting it into the DOM. Traditional insertion methods include appendChild (adds as last child) and insertBefore (inserts before a reference node). Modern methods - append, prepend, after, before - accept multiple arguments and text strings directly, making them more convenient. The replaceWith method replaces an element entirely.

Removing elements is straightforward: element.remove() removes the element from the DOM (modern), while parentNode.removeChild(child) is the older approach. When removing elements, be mindful of event listeners attached to them - listeners on removed elements are garbage collected if no other references exist, but delegated listeners on parent elements persist.

For batch operations, DocumentFragment is essential. A DocumentFragment is a lightweight, off-DOM container. You can append multiple elements to it without triggering reflows, then insert the entire fragment into the DOM in a single operation. This is significantly more performant than inserting elements one by one, as each individual DOM insertion can trigger the browser's layout engine.

Performance is critical when manipulating the DOM. Every time you modify the DOM, the browser may need to recalculate styles, compute layout (reflow), and repaint pixels on screen. Reading layout properties (offsetWidth, getBoundingClientRect) after writing to the DOM forces a synchronous reflow. The best practice is to batch all reads together and all writes together, minimize DOM access by caching references in variables, use DocumentFragment or innerHTML for bulk insertions, and prefer classList over direct style manipulation.

codeExamples.title: Selecting elements
codeExamples.explanation: querySelector uses CSS selector syntax, making it versatile for any selection pattern. querySelectorAll returns a static NodeList that does not update when the DOM changes, unlike getElementsBy* which return live collections.

codeExamples.title: Creating and inserting elements
codeExamples.explanation: createElement creates an element in memory. You configure it with properties and classes, then insert it into the DOM. The modern append/prepend/before/after methods are more flexible than appendChild/insertBefore.

codeExamples.title: DocumentFragment for batch operations
codeExamples.explanation: DocumentFragment acts as an invisible container. Elements are added to it without triggering layout recalculations. When the fragment is appended to the DOM, all its children are moved in one operation, causing only a single reflow.

codeExamples.title: Removing elements and innerHTML
codeExamples.explanation: element.remove() is the modern way to remove an element. For clearing children, replaceChildren() is the cleanest approach. innerHTML is fast for bulk HTML insertion but should never be used with untrusted content (XSS risk).

notes: textContent is safer and faster than innerHTML - it does not parse HTML, preventing XSS vulnerabilities.
classList provides add, remove, toggle, contains, and replace methods - prefer it over manipulating className directly.
Minimize DOM reads inside write loops - reading offsetWidth after changing styles forces a synchronous reflow (layout thrashing).
Modern frameworks like React use a Virtual DOM to batch and minimize real DOM updates automatically.
commonPitfalls: Using innerHTML with user-provided content - this is an XSS vulnerability. Use textContent or sanitize the HTML first.
Layout thrashing: alternating DOM reads and writes in a loop forces the browser to recalculate layout on every iteration.
Forgetting that querySelectorAll returns a NodeList, not an Array - use Array.from() or spread to get full array methods.
Not caching DOM references - calling querySelector repeatedly for the same element inside a loop is wasteful.

exercises.title: DOM Selector Methods
exercises.description: Without a real DOM, demonstrate your knowledge of DOM selection methods. Create an object that simulates which selector method you would use for different scenarios. For each scenario, log the correct method name as a string. Scenarios: (1) Select a single element by its ID 'header', (2) Select all elements with class 'card', (3) Select the first element matching a complex CSS selector 'nav > ul > li.active', (4) Select all paragraph elements inside a div with id 'content'.
exercises.expectedOutput:
By ID: document.getElementById
By class: document.getElementsByClassName
Complex selector: document.querySelector
Nested selector: document.querySelectorAll
querySelectorAll returns: static NodeList
hints: getElementById is the fastest way to select by ID. querySelector handles complex CSS selectors.
querySelectorAll returns a static NodeList (a snapshot), while getElementsByClassName returns a live HTMLCollection.

exercises.title: Build a DOM Tree in Memory
exercises.description: Simulate building a DOM-like tree structure using plain objects. Create a function buildCard(title, description) that returns an object representing a card element with the structure: { tag: 'div', classes: ['card'], children: [{ tag: 'h3', text: title }, { tag: 'p', text: description }] }. Build two cards and put them in a 'container' object. Log the container's children count, the first card's title text, and the second card's description text.
exercises.expectedOutput:
Children count: 2
First title: Welcome
Second desc: Learn more here
hints: The function should return an object with tag, classes array, and children array.
Each child is an object with tag and text properties representing h3 and p elements.

content.explanation:
Events are the mechanism through which JavaScript interacts with user actions and browser activities. Every click, keystroke, mouse movement, form submission, and page load fires an event that JavaScript can listen for and respond to. Understanding the event system deeply - including how events propagate through the DOM tree - is fundamental to building interactive web applications and is a frequently tested topic in interviews.

The primary way to attach event handlers is addEventListener(type, callback, options). The third argument can be a boolean (true for capture phase) or an options object with capture, once, passive, and signal properties. To remove a listener, call removeEventListener with the exact same function reference - this is why anonymous functions cannot be removed. The older onclick-style properties work but only allow one handler per event type.

When an event fires on an element, it does not just trigger on that element. It goes through three phases: capture phase (the event travels from the window down through ancestors to the target), target phase (the event reaches the actual element that was interacted with), and bubble phase (the event travels back up from the target through ancestors to the window). By default, addEventListener registers handlers for the bubble phase. Setting capture: true or passing true as the third argument registers for the capture phase instead.

The event object passed to handlers contains essential properties. event.target is the actual element that triggered the event (the deepest element clicked). event.currentTarget is the element the handler is attached to. event.type is the event name. event.stopPropagation() prevents the event from continuing to propagate. event.preventDefault() prevents the browser's default behavior (like form submission or link navigation). event.stopImmediatePropagation() also prevents other handlers on the same element from firing.

Event delegation is a pattern where you attach a single event listener to a parent element instead of individual listeners on each child. When an event bubbles up from a child, the parent's handler checks event.target to determine which child was clicked and responds accordingly. This has three major benefits: it works automatically with dynamically added elements, it uses less memory (one handler vs many), and it simplifies setup and teardown. Event delegation is especially powerful for lists, tables, and any container with many interactive children.

CustomEvent allows you to create and dispatch your own events with custom data. You create one with new CustomEvent('eventName', { detail: data, bubbles: true }) and dispatch it with element.dispatchEvent(customEvent). This is useful for component communication, plugin APIs, and decoupling parts of your application. Custom events follow the same capture/bubble propagation as native events.

codeExamples.title: addEventListener and event phases
codeExamples.output:
Outer - capture
Inner - capture
Button - bubble
Once!
Inner - bubble
Outer - bubble
codeExamples.explanation: Events travel down during capture (window to target), reach the target, then bubble back up (target to window). Capture handlers fire first, then bubble handlers. The 'once' option auto-removes the listener after one invocation.

codeExamples.title: event.target vs event.currentTarget
codeExamples.output:
target: LI
currentTarget: UL
this: UL
Link clicked but default behavior prevented
codeExamples.explanation: event.target is the element the user actually interacted with. event.currentTarget is the element the listener is attached to. preventDefault stops default browser behavior, stopPropagation stops further event propagation.

codeExamples.title: Event delegation pattern
codeExamples.output: (Delegation handles clicks on all current and future child elements)
codeExamples.explanation: A single listener on the parent handles events for all children by checking event.target. The matches() method tests if the target matches a CSS selector, and closest() finds the nearest ancestor matching a selector. This pattern works automatically for dynamically added elements.

codeExamples.title: Custom events
codeExamples.output:
Added 1x Laptop ($999) to cart
Global handler: cart updated
Added 2x Mouse ($29) to cart
Global handler: cart updated
codeExamples.explanation: CustomEvent allows you to create application-specific events with arbitrary data in the detail property. When bubbles is true, the event propagates up the DOM tree just like native events, enabling decoupled communication.

notes: Event delegation relies on event bubbling - it will not work for events that do not bubble (focus, blur, load, scroll on non-document targets). Use focusin/focusout as bubbling alternatives to focus/blur.
The passive: true option tells the browser that preventDefault will not be called, enabling scroll performance optimizations.
Arrow functions in addEventListener do not have their own 'this' - use regular functions if you need 'this' to refer to the element.
AbortController can be used with addEventListener's signal option to remove multiple listeners at once.
commonPitfalls: Adding listeners in a loop without delegation - each listener consumes memory and must be manually removed for cleanup.
Trying to removeEventListener with an anonymous function - you must pass the exact same function reference that was used in addEventListener.
Forgetting that stopPropagation prevents ALL parent handlers from firing - this can break event delegation on ancestor elements.
Using event.target without checking what was actually clicked - in delegation, target might be a nested child (like a <span> inside a <button>). Use closest() to find the intended element.
interviewTips: Be ready to explain the three event phases: capture (top-down), target, and bubble (bottom-up) with a diagram or code example.
Write an event delegation example from scratch - attach one listener to a parent <ul> that handles clicks on any <li>, including dynamically added ones.
Explain why event delegation is better for performance and dynamic content compared to individual listeners.
Know the difference between stopPropagation (stops propagation) and preventDefault (stops default browser behavior) - they are independent operations.

exercises.title: Event Propagation Order
exercises.description: Demonstrate your understanding of event propagation by predicting the order of event handler execution. Given a nested structure: document > div#outer > div#inner > button, with capture and bubble handlers on each, determine and log the correct order when the button is clicked. Log each phase as a string in the correct order.
exercises.expectedOutput:
document - capture
outer - capture
inner - capture
button - capture
button - bubble
inner - bubble
outer - bubble
document - bubble
hints: Capture phase goes top-down: document -> outer -> inner -> button.
Bubble phase goes bottom-up: button -> inner -> outer -> document. At the target element, capture runs before bubble.

exercises.title: Event Delegation Logic
exercises.description: Implement the logic of an event delegation handler without a real DOM. Write a function handleClick(targetClass, targetDataId) that simulates a delegated click handler for a todo list. If targetClass is 'delete-btn', log 'Delete item: ' + targetDataId. If targetClass is 'edit-btn', log 'Edit item: ' + targetDataId. If targetClass is 'toggle-btn', log 'Toggle item: ' + targetDataId. Otherwise log 'No action for: ' + targetClass. Test with all four cases.
exercises.expectedOutput:
Delete item: 42
Edit item: 7
Toggle item: 15
No action for: label
hints: In real event delegation, you use e.target.matches('.class') or e.target.classList.contains('class') to check what was clicked.
The function simulates the conditional logic inside a delegated event handler.

content.explanation:
Web storage APIs provide mechanisms for storing data in the browser. There are three main options: localStorage for persistent storage, sessionStorage for tab-scoped temporary storage, and cookies for small pieces of data that can be sent to the server with every HTTP request. Each has different characteristics regarding capacity, persistence, scope, and security, and choosing the right one depends on your use case.

localStorage provides persistent key-value storage that survives browser restarts. Data stored in localStorage has no expiration and remains until explicitly cleared by JavaScript or the user. It offers 5-10 MB of storage per origin (protocol + domain + port) and is synchronous - all operations block the main thread, though this is rarely noticeable for typical use. The API is simple: setItem(key, value), getItem(key), removeItem(key), clear(), and key(index). Values are always strings, so objects must be serialized with JSON.stringify() before storing and parsed with JSON.parse() when retrieved.

sessionStorage has the same API as localStorage but differs in scope and persistence. Data in sessionStorage is scoped to the browser tab - each tab has its own independent session storage even for the same origin. The data is cleared when the tab is closed. This makes sessionStorage ideal for temporary data like form progress, one-time tokens, or tab-specific UI state that should not persist across sessions or leak between tabs.

Cookies are the oldest browser storage mechanism, originally designed to send small pieces of data to the server with every HTTP request. They are limited to about 4KB per cookie and have additional attributes: expires or max-age for lifespan, path and domain for scope, secure to restrict to HTTPS, httpOnly to prevent JavaScript access (server-set only), and sameSite to control cross-site sending. While cookies are essential for session management and server-side authentication, localStorage is preferred for client-only data due to its larger capacity and simpler API.

Security is a critical concern with browser storage. localStorage and sessionStorage are vulnerable to Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) attacks - if an attacker injects malicious JavaScript into your page, they can read everything in storage. Never store sensitive tokens, passwords, or personal data in web storage without understanding this risk. Cookies with the httpOnly flag are safer for authentication tokens because JavaScript cannot access them, but they are vulnerable to Cross-Site Request Forgery (CSRF) unless the sameSite attribute is properly set.

When comparing the three options: use localStorage for user preferences, cached data, and non-sensitive state that should persist. Use sessionStorage for temporary data that should not survive tab closure. Use cookies for authentication tokens (with httpOnly and secure flags), server-needed data, and cross-subdomain sharing. For large amounts of structured data, consider IndexedDB, which offers asynchronous access and much larger storage limits.

codeExamples.title: localStorage basics
codeExamples.output:
dark
Alice
true
No token found
Items stored: 2
theme: dark
user: {"name":"Alice","age":25,"preferences":{"notifications":true}}
codeExamples.explanation: localStorage stores string key-value pairs persistently. Objects must be serialized with JSON.stringify before storing and deserialized with JSON.parse when retrieved. Data persists until explicitly removed.

codeExamples.title: sessionStorage and difference from localStorage
codeExamples.explanation: sessionStorage is scoped to the individual browser tab and cleared when the tab closes. This makes it ideal for temporary state like form progress that should not persist or leak between tabs.

codeExamples.title: Working with cookies
codeExamples.output:
username=Alice; theme=dark
Alice
dark
After deletion: { theme: 'dark' }
codeExamples.explanation: Cookies use a string-based API. document.cookie looks like a property but setting it adds/updates a single cookie rather than replacing all cookies. max-age sets expiration in seconds. httpOnly cookies can only be set by the server.

codeExamples.title: Storage comparison and safe wrapper
codeExamples.explanation: A wrapper handles JSON serialization, provides default values, catches quota errors, and handles unavailable storage gracefully. The 'storage' event fires when another tab modifies localStorage, enabling cross-tab communication.

notes: localStorage is synchronous - avoid storing very large amounts of data that could block the main thread.
The 'storage' event only fires in OTHER tabs/windows - not in the tab that made the change. This enables cross-tab communication.
In private/incognito mode, some browsers throw errors when accessing localStorage or limit its capacity to zero.
For structured data or large datasets, consider IndexedDB which is asynchronous and supports transactions.
commonPitfalls: Forgetting to JSON.stringify objects before storing - localStorage coerces values to strings, so an object becomes '[object Object]'.
Not wrapping localStorage access in try/catch - it throws in private browsing mode in some browsers and when quota is exceeded.
Storing sensitive data (tokens, passwords) in localStorage - it is accessible to any JavaScript on the page, making it vulnerable to XSS.
Assuming cookies are automatically sent with fetch requests - you must set credentials: 'include' or 'same-origin' in fetch options.

exercises.title: Storage API Simulator
exercises.description: Build a simple in-memory storage class called MockStorage that mimics the localStorage API. It should support setItem(key, value), getItem(key), removeItem(key), and a length getter. Internally use a plain object. Demonstrate that it stores values as strings by storing a number and checking its type on retrieval. Test all methods and log the results.
hints: localStorage always stores values as strings. Use String(value) in setItem to mimic this.
getItem should return null (not undefined) when the key does not exist, matching the real localStorage behavior.

exercises.title: Storage Comparison Table
exercises.description: Create an object that summarizes the key differences between localStorage, sessionStorage, and cookies. For each storage type, include: maxSize, persistence, scope, and sentToServer. Then log each storage type's properties. This tests your conceptual knowledge of Web Storage APIs.
exercises.expectedOutput:
localStorage: 5-10MB, permanent, per origin, server=false
sessionStorage: 5-10MB, tab close, per tab, server=false
cookies: 4KB, configurable, per domain, server=true
hints: localStorage and sessionStorage both have 5-10MB limits. Cookies are limited to about 4KB.
Only cookies are automatically sent with HTTP requests. localStorage and sessionStorage stay client-side.

content.explanation:
The Fetch API is the modern standard for making HTTP requests in JavaScript, replacing the older XMLHttpRequest. It provides a clean, promise-based interface that integrates naturally with async/await. Fetch is available in all modern browsers and in Node.js 18+, making it the universal choice for network requests.

The simplest fetch call is fetch(url), which makes a GET request and returns a Promise that resolves to a Response object. The Response object contains metadata about the response (status, headers, ok) and methods to read the body in different formats: .json() for JSON data, .text() for plain text, .blob() for binary data like images, .formData() for form data, and .arrayBuffer() for raw bytes. Each body method also returns a Promise and can only be called once - the body is a stream that is consumed on read.

For requests other than GET, you pass an options object as the second argument to fetch. This object can include method (GET, POST, PUT, PATCH, DELETE), headers (an object or Headers instance), body (the request payload - typically JSON.stringify(data) for JSON APIs), mode (cors, no-cors, same-origin), credentials (omit, same-origin, include for cookies), and signal (for request cancellation). When sending JSON, you must set the Content-Type header to 'application/json' - fetch does not do this automatically.

Error handling with fetch has an important nuance: fetch only rejects the promise on network errors (DNS failure, no internet, CORS blocked). HTTP error responses like 404 or 500 are considered successful fetches - the promise resolves normally. You must check response.ok (true for status 200-299) or response.status manually to detect HTTP errors. This is different from libraries like axios that throw on non-2xx status codes.

AbortController provides a mechanism to cancel fetch requests. You create a controller with new AbortController(), pass controller.signal to the fetch options, and call controller.abort() to cancel. The fetch promise rejects with an AbortError. This is essential for cleanup in React components (preventing state updates after unmount), implementing request timeouts, and canceling stale search-as-you-type requests. AbortController can cancel multiple fetches simultaneously by passing the same signal to each.

Comparing fetch to alternatives: XMLHttpRequest is the legacy API with an event-based interface and upload progress support. Axios is a popular library that adds automatic JSON parsing, request/response interceptors, automatic error throwing on non-2xx responses, and cancellation (now using AbortController too). For most use cases, fetch with a small wrapper function provides everything you need without a dependency.

codeExamples.title: GET request and response handling
codeExamples.output:
Leanne Graham
Sincere@april.biz
Status: 200
OK: true
Content-Type: application/json; charset=utf-8
Posts count: 100
codeExamples.explanation: fetch returns a Response object. Always check response.ok before reading the body, since fetch does not throw on HTTP errors. The .json() method parses the response body as JSON and returns a promise.

codeExamples.title: POST, PUT, DELETE requests
codeExamples.output:
Created: { title: 'Hello', body: 'World', userId: 1, id: 101 }
Deleted: true
codeExamples.explanation: For POST/PUT, set the Content-Type header and pass the data as a JSON string in the body. DELETE requests typically do not need a body. Always check response.ok for error detection.

codeExamples.title: Error handling: network vs HTTP errors
codeExamples.explanation: This wrapper distinguishes between network errors (TypeError - no connection), cancellation (AbortError), and HTTP errors (non-2xx status codes). Fetch only throws on network failures, so HTTP errors must be detected manually via response.ok.

codeExamples.title: AbortController for cancellation
codeExamples.output:
Search for "al" was cancelled
Search for "ali" was cancelled
Results for "alice": 10
codeExamples.explanation: AbortController lets you cancel in-flight fetch requests. This is essential for search-as-you-type (cancel stale requests), request timeouts, and React cleanup (cancel requests when a component unmounts).

notes: fetch is natively available in all modern browsers and Node.js 18+. No polyfill or library needed.
Response body can only be read once - calling .json() a second time throws. Clone the response with response.clone() if you need to read it multiple times.
Set credentials: 'include' to send cookies with cross-origin requests, or 'same-origin' for same-origin only.
The Headers API provides methods like headers.get(), headers.has(), headers.set(), and is iterable with for...of.
commonPitfalls: Assuming fetch throws on 404 or 500 - it does not. You must check response.ok or response.status manually.
Forgetting to set Content-Type: application/json when sending JSON - the server may not parse the body correctly without it.
Not handling AbortError separately in catch blocks - it is not a real error, just a cancellation signal.
Using fetch without awaiting the body method - response.json() returns a Promise, not the parsed data directly.

exercises.title: Fetch Error Handling Logic
exercises.description: Without making real HTTP requests, write a function classifyFetchError(statusCode) that simulates how you should handle different HTTP status codes returned by fetch. For status 200-299, log 'Success: OK'. For 400, log 'Client Error: Bad Request'. For 401, log 'Client Error: Unauthorized'. For 404, log 'Client Error: Not Found'. For 500, log 'Server Error: Internal Server Error'. For any other status, log 'Unknown status: ' + the code. Remember: fetch does NOT throw for these -- you must check manually. Test with status codes 200, 401, 404, and 500.
exercises.expectedOutput:
Success: OK
Client Error: Unauthorized
Client Error: Not Found
Server Error: Internal Server Error
hints: response.ok is true for status 200-299. Everything else requires manual checking.
Use if/else if chains or a switch statement to handle each status code category.

exercises.title: Build a Fetch Options Object
exercises.description: Write a function buildFetchOptions(method, data, token) that returns a properly structured options object for the fetch API. For GET requests, it should only include the method and authorization header. For POST/PUT, it should also include Content-Type: application/json and a JSON stringified body. Test it with a GET request (no data, with token), a POST request (with data and token), and log the results.
exercises.expectedOutput:
GET method: GET
GET auth: Bearer abc123
GET has body: false
POST method: POST
POST content-type: application/json
POST body: {"title":"Hello"}
hints: GET requests should not have a body. Only add body and Content-Type for methods that send data.
Use JSON.stringify(data) for the body and set Content-Type to 'application/json'.
